notes: Skill bumps pass rate from 73.2% to 100% with zero variance across 3 evals. The two baselines that failed assertions both stopped short of executing lesson 1 (k8s baseline) or persisting a curriculum file (all three baselines). The skill's value is enforcing the *full* workflow (intake -> plan -> save -> execute lesson), not the content of any single step.

Baselines are unusually strong: each one grounded in real files, included alternatives/comparison sections, and asked clarifying questions. This is partly because Claude defaults are already aligned with good tutoring shape - and partly because the skill description was visible in the available-skills list to the baseline subagents (methodological caveat worth noting).

Single-largest differentiator: file persistence. All three baselines failed the 'curriculum saved to outputs/curriculum.md' assertion. Two of three explicitly noted they *would* have saved to ~/.claude/mentor-curricula/ but the sandbox refused. This is a real-world environmental artifact for the test setup, not a skill defect - production usage would not hit it (and even the with-skill agents hit the same sandbox issue for the canonical path, but they at least wrote to the workspace copy).

Skill is ~2.3x slower (327s vs 144s mean) and uses ~1.5x tokens. The added cost is primarily the executor following the full workflow (more reading to ground, intake + plan + lesson, file writes). Acceptable tradeoff given the pass-rate jump.

Assertion that always passes regardless of skill: 'Curriculum references real file paths.' Both configurations did this confidently. Non-discriminating - consider replacing with a tighter check (e.g., 'at least 3 file refs include line numbers and link to verified existing files').

k8s baseline diverged most sharply: 0.571 pass rate vs 1.0 with-skill. The lesson-1 execution assertions (grounds-then-generalizes, comprehension check) were the entire delta - the baseline didn't run lesson 1 at all, stopping at 'waiting for user confirmation'. The skill explicitly instructs to proceed through plan -> approval -> lesson 1 as one motion when the user opens with 'run the first lesson too', so this is exactly the kind of contract the skill is enforcing.
